title: Class Variables 🗃️
description: Global data that is seamlessly shared across all instances.

slides.title: The Global Counter ⏱️
slides.content:
What if you wanted to build 100 `Dog` objects, and dynamically keep track of EXACTLY how many dogs exist across your entire program?

You can't use an Instance Variable (`@count`), because that is trapped privately inside every single dog.

You need a **Class Variable**.

slides.title: Two At Symbols @@
slides.content:
A Class Variable belongs to the blueprint, but its data is openly shared across all of its child objects.

You define a Class Variable exactly like an instance variable, but you use TWO completely glued-together at symbols `@@`.

`@@dog_counts = 0`

slides.title: Tracking the Total
slides.content: Run this brilliant logic! Every single time the constructor (`initialize`) runs during a `.new` call, the globally shared `@@count` goes up cleanly by one.
slides.codeSnippet:
class Dog
  @@total_dogs = 0
  
  def initialize(name)
    @@total_dogs += 1
    @name = name
  end
  
  def self.report_count
    puts "Total dogs created: #{@@total_dogs}"
  end
end

a = Dog.new("Fido")
b = Dog.new("Buddy")
c = Dog.new("Sparky")

Dog.report_count

quizData.question: Which prefix explicitly denotes a Class Variable that is securely shared across every single instance built from the Class?
quizData.explanation: Remember: `@` is for private Instance data. `@@` is for shared Class data.

slides.title: Danger Zone ⚠️
slides.content:
While Class Variables are cool, professional developers rarely use them.

Why? Because if you have complex code with subclasses, updating `@@shared_data` in one place can accidentally permanently overwrite it for everyone else and cause huge logic bugs!

Just stick to standard `@instance` variables 99% of the time.
